Title: L-Ascorbate biosynthesis in higher plants: the role of VTC2

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; supports plant growth, stress protection, and human nutrition
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf; chloroplast (plant cellular context discussed broadly)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Encodes a specific L-galactose-1-P phosphatase contributing to L-ascorbate biosynthesis; knockout is only partially deficient in L-ascorbate, indicating partial contribution.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: ascorbate-deficient mutant identification; knockout analysis; enzyme activity assay
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: mRNA correlated with light-responsive changes in ascorbate, but enzyme activity unchanged under such conditions
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTC4 encodes a specific L-galactose-1-P phosphatase in the L-galactose pathway. 2. VTC4 knockout causes only partial L-ascorbate deficiency, implying additional enzyme(s) can catalyze this step. 3. VTC4 transcript levels correlate with light-induced ascorbate changes, but enzyme activity does not clearly change.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary engineering target for vitamin C enhancement, but likely insufficient alone because this step is partially redundant.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Limited impact expected due to pathway redundancy; effect on cell phosphate metabolism not established.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AtPAP15 was proposed as a possible additional enzyme for this reaction, but L-galactose-1-P substrate usage was not tested in the paper.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; tobacco; Escherichia coli expression system
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Escherichia coli
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; oxidative stress protection; nutritional vitamin C source
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf; chloroplast in general plant context
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: committed branch from GDP-L-galactose to L-galactose-1-P
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts GDP-L-galactose and inorganic phosphate to L-galactose-1-P and GDP via a guanylylated His intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate committed-step intermediate toward L-ascorbate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes L-ascorbate biosynthesis; loss-of-function causes vitamin C deficiency, while overexpression of a homolog increases ascorbate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant mapping; recombinant enzyme assay; HPLC-based activity assay; active-site guanylylation evidence; mutant rescue with L-galactose or L-ascorbate; transient overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expressed in leaf, stem, root, flower, and silique; induced by high light; possibly circadian regulated; induced by jasmonates; repressed by L-ascorbate supplementation at transcript level
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: high-light exposure increases VTC2 transcript and GDP-L-galactose phosphorylase activity in Arabidopsis; kiwifruit homolog transiently overexpressed in tobacco leaves
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: high light; jasmonate treatment; L-ascorbate supplementation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTC2 was identified as the long-sought GDP-L-galactose phosphorylase catalyzing the first committed step of the L-galactose pathway. 2. Biochemical evidence supports phosphorylase activity with inorganic phosphate as the major physiological reaction, far exceeding guanylyltransferase activity. 3. VTC2 expression is strongly induced by high light and jasmonates and reduced by exogenous L-ascorbate, linking it to pathway regulation. 4. vtc2 mutations reduce ascorbate, and combined loss with VTC5 is seedling lethal unless rescued by L-galactose or L-ascorbate. 5. Overexpression of a kiwifruit homolog in tobacco increases ascorbate about threefold, supporting rate limitation at this step.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for biofortification of vitamin C; overexpression or promoter engineering under light/developmental control may elevate foliar or fruit ascorbate; stacking with downstream pathway genes may improve flux and stability.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible perturbation of GDP-sugar allocation between ascorbate synthesis and cell wall/glycoprotein biosynthesis; complete loss causes severe growth defects when combined with VTC5.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Can also use GDP-D-glucose and GDP-L-fucose as substrates in vitro; D-mannose-1-P transferase-based VTC2 cycle remains controversial and likely not dominant in plants.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; oxidative stress protection; nutritional vitamin C source
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf; chloroplast in general plant context
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: committed branch from GDP-L-galactose to L-galactose-1-P
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts GDP-L-galactose and inorganic phosphate to L-galactose-1-P and GDP, with kinetics resembling VTC2.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate committed-step intermediate toward L-ascorbate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts redundantly with VTC2 in committed ascorbate biosynthesis; single mutants show little effect but double loss with VTC2 abolishes viability unless rescued by pathway intermediates/products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: sequence homology; recombinant enzyme assay; T-DNA mutant analysis; double-mutant rescue with L-galactose or L-ascorbate
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expressed in leaf, stem, root, flower, and silique; generally 100-1000 fold lower than VTC2; induced by high light and jasmonates; possibly circadian regulated
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Arabidopsis vtc5 single mutants show near-wild-type leaf ascorbate; high light increases VTC5 transcript
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTC5 is a second Arabidopsis GDP-L-galactose phosphorylase highly similar to VTC2. 2. Its catalytic properties resemble VTC2 and it predominantly acts as a phosphorylase rather than guanylyltransferase. 3. VTC5 is expressed much more weakly than VTC2, explaining the mild phenotype of vtc5 single mutants. 4. VTC2 and VTC5 together are essential for Arabidopsis seedling viability and for the major source of ascorbate production.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful companion target with VTC2 for stronger vitamin C biofortification and redundancy buffering; may be combined in multigene engineering strategies.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Functional redundancy may mask effects of single-gene manipulation; dual impairment causes severe bleaching and arrested growth.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper did not analyze VTC5 subcellular localization.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; nutritional vitamin C source
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaf; chloroplast in general plant context
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Forms GDP-D-mannose for entry into the L-galactose pathway and for cell wall/glycoprotein biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced VTC1 activity lowers L-ascorbate content.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant mapping; antisense inhibition; correlation of mRNA levels with ascorbate; hormone induction studies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: induced by jasmonates; mRNA correlated with ascorbate in several plant species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTC1 encodes GDP-D-mannose pyrophosphorylase upstream of the committed ascorbate branch. 2. Arabidopsis vtc1 mutants and potato antisense lines show lowered L-ascorbate. 3. VTC1 expression correlates with ascorbate levels and is induced by jasmonates. 4. Because GDP-D-mannose also feeds cell wall and glycoprotein pathways, its regulatory significance for ascorbate may be shared with competing processes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be considered for precursor-pool enhancement, but engineering must account for competing demands in wall biosynthesis and glycosylation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering GDP-D-mannose supply may affect cell wall formation and protein glycosylation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as an upstream control point rather than the major decisive step.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; nutritional vitamin C source
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epimerizes GDP-D-mannose to GDP-L-galactose and GDP-L-gulose.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor and may initiate alternative branch
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Influences precursor supply for L-ascorbate biosynthesis and may open an alternative L-gulose route.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: biochemical characterization; metabolite modulation studies; expression induction by methyl jasmonate
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expression induced by methyl jasmonate in tobacco cells
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GME produces both GDP-L-galactose and GDP-L-gulose from GDP-D-mannose. 2. This dual activity suggests a branch point between the major L-galactose pathway and a proposed L-gulose pathway. 3. Metabolite modulation and jasmonate-responsive expression suggest possible regulatory importance.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential precursor/branch-control engineering target, especially if alternative ascorbate routes are validated.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter partitioning of GDP-sugars needed for other essential processes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper emphasizes possible but unresolved contribution to alternative L-gulose biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; nutritional vitamin C source
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Interconverts D-mannose-6-P and D-mannose-1-P to support GDP-D-mannose biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Decreased PMM transcript levels or mutation lowers L-ascorbate content.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: virus-induced gene silencing; transgenic expression; mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PMM contributes to precursor generation upstream of ascorbate synthesis. 2. Lower PMM expression or mutation reduces L-ascorbate in Nicotiana benthamiana and/or Arabidopsis. 3. These findings suggest PMM can influence overall pathway flux.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for increasing precursor availability, but likely less effective than manipulating the committed step.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Upstream perturbation may affect broader mannose metabolism and cell wall-related pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Presented as a possible regulatory site rather than the main focus.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; enzyme cofactor; nutritional vitamin C source
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes L-galactono-1,4-lactone to L-ascorbate using cytochrome c as electron acceptor in the respiratory chain.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: not established; may contribute under light/respiratory regulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Correlates with ascorbate content in many tissues, but RNAi reduction in tomato decreased growth without reducing ascorbate synthesis capacity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transcript/activity correlation; RNA interference; enzyme specificity characterization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: transcript and activity often positively correlate with ascorbate across tissues; possible light regulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GLDH catalyzes the last step of ascorbate biosynthesis in mitochondria. 2. Its activity often correlates with ascorbate content, and it may be influenced by light and respiration. 3. However, reduced GLDH activity in tomato did not lower ascorbate synthesis capacity, arguing against strong flux control under all conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible context-dependent target, especially where mitochondrial/redox regulation is relevant, but not clearly a robust standalone engineering lever.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNAi reduction caused decreased growth rates in tomato.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Possible existence of multiple GLDH isoforms, including forms that may use L-gulono-1,4-lactone.
